utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hey jose how are you doing it's been about you know three or four months since i last saw you what's going on

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'm doing okay i i guess the medications are helping i'm concerned about all the medications i'm taking but i think i'm doing okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah yeah you are on a fair amount of medications you know we do have you on those medications because you did have a heart attack about six months ago they put that that stent into your left anterior descending artery and so that's a major artery on the left side of your heart and so we need to keep those stents open and and kinda help your heart heal so it is gon na be very important for you to continue on those medications so you're still taking your aspirin and are you still taking the brilinta

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes and and fish oil

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and fish oil okay good now how are you doing with the crestor i know that that's you know that's the statin that's the cholesterol medication you were concerned that it might cause you to have some muscle aches are you taking that

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i i think i'm doing good but i i am i feel like i i when i i guess the only complaint that i have is ever since i i do n't know if this is the heart attack or if this this is the the medication but when i stand up real fast i get really dizzy

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you get you get dizzy okay yeah you know we do have you on a what we call a beta-blocker metoprolol i think your dose is fifty milligrams once a day and you know sometimes it takes a little bit for your body to adjust to that but yes if you stand up too quickly it can cause you to be a little dizzy so

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay okay that makes sense okay that's good to know because i did n't know what what what the problem was so can i ask you a question regarding the stents

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yep

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: how long do i have to have that in there

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so the stents are permanent we do n't we do n't remove them so yep so that's why it's gon na be super important for you you know you're gon na be on aspirin for the rest of your life and then i'm gon na keep you on that brilinta for another six months that's gon na help prevent the that stent from clogging

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay okay do i need to ever replace the stents then

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: no i mean every so often the stents can become narrowed again but you know that's why you're seeing me your cardiologist so that i can follow you for that now if you start to have symptoms again of any chest pain like you had before or any shortness of breath when you start to exert yourself i want you to tell me and we might have to go ahead and send you for further testing to make sure that those stents are okay but typically if you continue to take the medication watch your diet that type of thing your stents will will stay open for a long time

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay alright that sounds good you know i have n't i had a i had i know you asked me to follow up with the dietitian because i had diabetes as well which probably contributed to my heart attack

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i may need a new order i do n't i i forgot who i was supposed to talk to

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay that's okay yeah i certainly can help you with that now managing your diabetes is also going to play an important role in your coronary artery disease which it sounds like you already know that which is really good so i will put in another referral for diabetic education for you how are you doing watching your sweets i know that you know you do have an afinity for those chocolate chip cookies

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it's okay my a1c was n't where i wanted but my glucose is coming in in about one thirty

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: fasting

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay what was the what was the a1c the hemoglobin a1c if you do n't mind me asking

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i thought it was close to sixty

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay yeah that's really high so we want it to be about six so you you do have some work to do with your diet

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah yeah so okay so i know you asked me to do a blood test so i did that i was wondering if you got the results

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i did you know i looked at the results of your kidney function that's what we call your creatinine just because you had some contrast dye and sometimes contrast dye can impact the kidneys and your kidney function is stable which is great when was the last time you saw your nephrologist

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: oh

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that's your kidney doctor

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'm a bad i'm a bad patient so probably a year ago

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay you know you do have the stage three ckd so that's the the stage three kidney disease so it is gon na be important for you to follow up with your nephrologist just to make sure that everything is okay now i did check your kidney function and and it was fine but are you staying away from things like motrin and advil

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: right yeah that's i threw all of those out

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay good okay perfect now i know that you're from the west coast are you super excited that la one the super bowl or are you more of a san francisco forty nine or span

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'm a more of a san francisco fan

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so do you like jimmy g do you think he can beat erin rogers again

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay we'll see we'll see what happens but i'm pretty flexible when he comes to you know that is but one thing that since i ca n't take motrin what pain killer can i take for headaches

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you can take tylenol

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: alright that sounds good

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: this is i i do n't think i have any other question the only thing i'm gon na need is a i need some refills on my medications

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah i can do that but i do wan na just do a physical exam on you okay so and looking here at your vital signs here you know your your vital signs look quite good you know your blood pressure is what right where it should be it's about one twenty seven over eighty which is great so you're doing a good job taking your metoprolol now in terms of on your neck exam i do n't appreciate any jugular venous distention or carotid bruits on your heart exam i do appreciate a slight three out of six systolic ejection murmur heard at the left base and on your lower extremity exam i do appreciate some one plus nonpitting edema now what does that mean what does that mean jose so all of that means is that i do hear a little heart murmur on your heart which i've heard before in the past and it does n't it does n't look like you're retaining any fluid which is good but you do have a little puffiness in your legs which sometimes some of the medications can cause

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i do have that

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so let's just go over a little bit about my assessment and and plan for you going forward so for your first problem of your coronary artery disease i think you're doing really well i wan na go ahead and refer you for cardiac rehab just so that you can be more active and have some confidence in in exercising again okay and i am gon na go ahead and refill your metoprolol your crestor forty milligrams once a day as well as the aspirin okay i do n't think we need to proceed with a a stress test or anything at this time but you know to let me know if you have any symptoms okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay for your

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sounds really good

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: good for your second problem of your stage three chronic kidney disease i am gon na reach out to doctor miller and make an appointment for you to be seen just because again i think it's important for you to follow up with your nephrologist okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and then for your third problem of your diabetes i am gon na go ahead and order another hemoglobin a1c and i'm gon na put that referral in for the dietitian to give you some dietary education and i'm gon na go ahead and talk with your primary care physician about your your insulin regimen okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: any questions

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah you know i've been kinda down ever since i had my heart attack is there any medication that i could take to make me feel a little bit less sad

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah i mean are you having any thoughts of of harming yourself or harming others

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no no i just feel kinda down

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you feel kinda down what's your

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: life sucks

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: what well i do n't think so what what's your support system like

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you know i i live with my dog and that's it so it's kind of you know lonely

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright well would you be interested in in like a referral to psychiatry or something like that so that you have someone to talk to about some of of issues because medication can help but i do i think it's important to have you know talk some of these things out

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay yeah you know i'll give that a try i mean i'm retiring i got plenty of time

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so why do n't we go ahead and i'm gon na place a referral to psychiatry for you know you know difficulty adjusting after having a heart attack maybe some some mild depression okay does that sound okay okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah that sounds really good thank you

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay you're welcome anything else

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so you will do the medication refill do i so that's automatic

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yep i'm gon na put them into the computer and and whatever pharmacy you want yep mm-hmm

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay alright sounds great thank you

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay you're welcome i'll be in touch bye

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay alright bye-bye

detail_list.summary: Follow-up visit.
The patient had a heart attack six months ago and received a stent in the left anterior descending artery.
The doctor asks if the patient is still taking aspirin and Brilinta.
The patient confirms taking aspirin, Brilinta, and fish oil.
The doctor asks if the patient is taking Crestor and if he is experiencing muscle aches.
The patient reports dizziness when standing up quickly.
The doctor notes the patient is taking metoprolol 50 mg once a day and explains that dizziness upon standing can be a side effect.
Stents
The patient asks how long the stents will remain in place.
The doctor explains that stents are permanent and the patient must continue aspirin for life and Brilinta for another six months.
The patient asks if the stents need to be replaced.
The doctor states stents do not typically need replacement but may narrow, and advises the patient to report chest pain or shortness of breath.
Diabetes
The patient asks for a new referral to a dietitian.
The doctor agrees to provide a referral for diabetic education.
The patient reports a fasting glucose of 130.
The doctor asks for the patient's most recent hemoglobin A1c.
The patient reports an A1c of 16.
The doctor states the A1c goal is 6.
The patient asks if the doctor received the results of the blood test.
The doctor confirms the creatinine levels are stable and asks when the patient last saw their nephrologist.
The patient reports not seeing the nephrologist for a year.
The doctor advises the patient to follow up with the nephrologist for stage 3 CKD and asks if the patient is avoiding Motrin and Advil.
The patient confirms they have discarded Motrin and Advil.
The patient asks what painkiller they can take for headaches instead of Motrin.
The doctor recommends Tylenol.
The patient requests medication refills.
The doctor performs a physical exam, noting blood pressure 127/80, no JVD or carotid bruits, a 3/6 systolic ejection murmur, and 1+ non-pitting edema.
The doctor plans to refer the patient to cardiac rehab and refill metoprolol, Crestor 40 mg once a day, and aspirin.
The doctor will schedule an appointment with Dr. Miller for the patient's stage 3 CKD.
The doctor will order a repeat A1c, refer to a dietitian, and contact the primary care physician regarding the insulin regimen.
Depression
The patient asks if there is medication for feeling sad.
The doctor asks if the patient has thoughts of harming themselves or others.
The patient denies suicidal or homicidal ideation.
The patient reports living alone with a dog and feeling lonely.
The doctor asks if the patient is interested in a psychiatry referral.
The doctor will place a referral to psychiatry for mild depression.
